text: robotic-assisted colotomy for removal of a benign polyp of the colon. We present a case of a seventy-one year old female who presented with a large polyp of the transverse colon. She had a colonoscopy for anemia and they found a four centimeter polyp in the proximal transverse colon. Partial piecemeal polypectomy was performed and revealed a tubulovilous adenoma without dysplasia or malignancy. To completely remove the lesion, she was referred to GI for endoscopic mucous resection. This, however, was unsuccessful due to the inability to elevate the lesion completely from the submucosal plane.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative colonoscopy to identify the lesion marked by a previous tattoo. Attempts are made to elevate the lesion using a submucosal injection of saline, which proves difficult. Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected to aid in precise localization of the lesion.

Using fluorescence imaging, the surgeon identifies the anti-mesenteric border of the colon corresponding to the polyp's location. A longitudinal colotomy is made over the anti-mesenteric tinea, and ICG fluorescence angiography confirms the polyp's precise location. The colotomy is expanded along the longitudinal border, allowing for clear identification of the lesion. The surgeon elevates the polyp and excises it using monopolar energy and sharp dissection, ensuring gross negative borders. The robotic platform aids in close visualization and precise maneuvering of scissors and grasper, resulting in intact excision with negative radial and deep margins.

After excising the polyp, the surgeon introduces a 5mm endo bag through a robotic port to retrieve the specimen. The polypectomy site is prepared for closure using a continuous 3-0 V-lock suture with a V-20 needle. The robotic instruments facilitate consistent tissue bites and continuous motion, allowing the surgeon to lift the tissue by gently pulling on the suture. Full thickness bites are taken, and the suture is reloaded without dropping the needle, ensuring efficient closure from the polypectomy site to the corner. Additional bites are taken in reverse to lock the suture.

The surgeon closes the colotomy using a three-oh running V-lock suture with the robotic platform. The suture is gently tucked in, lifting and re-engaging the tissue, ensuring the bite closes transversely using the needle tip. The suturing is performed in a zipper line fashion, and upon reaching the corner of the defect, the motion is reversed to lock the suture. The suture line is inspected, and any gaps are reinforced with interrupted Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon performs a vinyl colonoscopy to verify that the colon lumen is not narrowed and is appropriately closed. The robotic-assisted colotomy provides excellent visualization and access for the identification and excision of a large polyp in the colon.
